Computer. Hi, this is David Deutsch and I'm here with Sam Woods, who is my favorite expert on AI in relation to all things marketing and copy, and as well as other things. Because Sam is someone who's been using AI for the past three years, who's been evangelical about it in terms of, hey, Dave, you really ought to look into this thing. It's gonna be big one day. And it's gonna be important. It's gonna change everything. And I was like, okay, Sam, I'll look into it a little bit, but kind of like, okay. But then overnight, it's kind of happened. And I took Sam's course, which was great in AI and copywriting. And I wanna talk to him today about AI and creativity and how to use it to come up with ideas. So Sam, welcome. And what are your thoughts about using AI? Can AI come up with ideas for us? Good to talk to you, David. I appreciate you having me on and speaking with you about this. I'm glad people are listening. So there's been a lonely few voices in the desert in the wilderness crying out for a couple of years, but that's okay. It's all good. AI is probably the best tool right now. And I'm gonna say is that I need to qualify a few things. So AI is probably the best tool for creativity that's available right now. And maybe that'll be the best tool for the foreseeable future. But to me, and with the practical experience I've had with it for years now, there is no better tool for creativity. And to help us be creative and come up with ideas and brainstorm and combine and rewrite and redo, there is no better tool. But when I say AI, like it's important people understand artificial intelligence. When I say that it's not artificial intelligence the way it is traditionally understood or the way people have thought about it and talked about it for whatever the past 100 years and science fiction, everything else. That's more like there are no, there are no sentient robots walking around down the street. And when you interact with chat GPT or GPT-3, it's not sentient, it doesn't actually understand. It's just a very good predictor of text that should come after whatever you give it. And so what we're really talking about is machine learning. So these bots like chat GPT and GPT-3 and all the tools that are based off of those like Jasper uses it, copy.ai uses it. All these text tools out there uses GPT-3 via API to do the things. But it's not artificial intelligence. The way we understand it to be is not artificial general intelligence, which is maybe someday the machines will be knowledgeable about themselves and have self-awareness. It's really machine learning and a data set that's been trained to produce outcomes via machine learning. So the real, the proper way to understand is that we're leveraging the power of machine learning, the outcomes of machine learning to help us with creativity. And when you put in text into a prompt into any of these tools, what it's doing really fast and really well is that it's predicting the next words that should come after. So it looks to us as if we're interacting with a bot. It looks to us as if it thinks, understands, writes and expresses. It looks like magic, but it's not magic. In other words, it does not understand really what we're asking, nor does it understand what its reply is. It's just really good and really fast at knowing if I see these words, generally these type of words satisfy these words and go as a answer, you have a response to that. And it connects to the clusters of topics that it knows is related to whatever you're saying. So if I ask about putting banana on pizza as a topping, then it'll connect the dots, so to speak, with banana, pizza, baking, making pizza. And so if I then ask, what's the history of putting banana on pizza, it'll go back into its understanding of those topics and find the answer and then give me the answer. So in some sense, it's almost like Google on steroids because it can find the answers, but in another sense, it's also not like Google because it's not a search engine. Like you use it to search because it formulates answers and puts together answers to us that we understand. But again, it has no clue. It has no clue what banana is. It has no clue what pizza is. It just finds the relevant topic, the relevant subtopics, the relevant words, the relevant expressions, and then give it to me. And if I ask it to give it to me as a story about a pizza maker who discovers how to put banana on pizza, it'll give me a story about a pizza maker, baker, whatever they're called. But this person, one day discovering how to put banana on pizza, it does not understand that it gave me a story, but it knows what a story is in terms of how to formulate an answer that looks like a story. Does that make sense? I've seen thousands of stories before. Millions, millions. Oh, I get it. I can put this into this format and that will satisfy this. And then I can ask it to rewrite it as a poem as a Shakespearean sonnet. And it'll rewrite the story into the form of a Shakespearean sonnet versus an Italian sonnet, for example, right? So in this sense, it understands, so I'm using anthropomorphic language to describe it just so people get what's happening to a degree. At the same time, it's not accurate for me to say that it understands, right? Like it just, it predicts the text. Still. Yeah, go ahead. I was just gonna say, so how does this help us to be more creative then? Because it's a game of association, essentially. It's a game of association and connecting dots and coming up with variants related to whatever the core question is, that you can sit and ask it the same thing with slight variations and just click, like enter all the time. And you will get an endless inexhaustible stream of ideas, related concepts, related anything. Like if I say banana on pizza as a topic, and then I say, give me 20 metaphors, analogies and scientific sounding terms for this idea of banana on pizza, and it'll give me 20 metaphors, analogies or scientific sounding terms for the idea of banana pizza, and it can do so inexhaustibly. As a human, eventually we run out of, like our mind gets tired, we get tired after doing that for a whole day, after thinking for a whole day, it consumes energy, and even if you're nutrition and diet and water drinking is where it should be, and so you're fueled up, just biologically speaking, your brain will run out of energy from doing that for a whole day. But chatGPT or GPT-3 can do so endlessly, and it can keep giving you new things. And what happens is that it'll give you new ideas, new topics, new associations, new whatever endlessly, and you'll find in that output more unique ideas that you would have, it may take you a while to come up with the same ideas, or you would never come up with the ideas that chatGPT gives you. I think also too, one of the things I teach in creativity is that any idea can trigger another idea in your own brain. So it may be that you use, it comes out with five ideas that you don't use, but one of those ideas makes you think in a different direction that makes you come up with a brilliant idea. Exactly, and what you get is usually, like it's a process, creativity is a process, it's not an event, even though we like to think of it as, oh, Eureka, you get an idea all of a sudden, but that's not where the creativity happens, it's a process. And in the same way when you interact with chatGPT, it's a process and a dialogue that you have with the bot for constantly asking for more, asking for expansions, asking for more detail. And so if I ask for, if I start very generic, and I say, give me 20 ideas for how I, give me 20 recipes for how I can put banana on pizza. And then it'll give me like 20 recipes on how to do it. Maybe recipe number 11, I go, oh wow, banana combined with all these other ingredients on a pizza baked a certain way. That's really interesting. So you take idea number 11, and you put it back into the prompt, and then you ask things like, what's the history of pizza recipes? What's the history of putting fruit on pizza? It's like all of the world's encyclopedic knowledge, whatever the word is, is available at your fingertips, and you can just start going down rabbit trails for exploring any given topic, and keep asking questions of what you're looking for. Even if you don't know what you're looking for, you just ask like, give me 10 creative ideas for how to put banana on pizza. And if you, those, and maybe idea number five sets you off another trail, where you explore history, pain points, interests, avatars of different kinds, popular topics that involve banana on pizza, TV shows, books that involve banana on pizza, it's endless as to where you can go, and it just keeps going. One of the things, I think it was you that I saw do this, was to insert, it came up with these ideas, and then you said, okay, try doing some ideas around the science fiction theme now. And then all of a sudden, it came up with all these neat ideas about blast into outer space, or, you know, go where no one has gone before, like the intro to Star Trek, it started making Star Trek and Star Wars analogies and cool ways of combining things. Mm-hmm. Yeah, it's great. So creativity often happens when you put two widely different concepts together, or topics or categories together, or even three or four things, like you combine, and in that combination, stuff happens. And chatGPT and GPT-3 are excellent for you saying, combine this category with this category, with this topic, around this other topic, and talk to me about this topic A, in light of topic B, C, and D. And then it'll jam these ideas together, and express topic A in like science fiction terms, in therapeutic terms, in fashion terms, in art terms, whatever worlds you want to combine, and then give it a focal point. It'll do it, and it'll, if you ask for metaphors, it'll give you metaphor. If you ask for analogies, it'll give you analogies. If you ask for scientific sounding terms, it'll give you that. If you ask for nicknames, it'll give you that, and it'll just combine all of it into an output. And however, for however long you want to keep going down that road, you just keep asking it for more, and it'll endlessly give you more combinations. I teach people a series of prompts about, I think of them as boxes, right? Thinking inside the box, right? So it's things like, do the opposite, add to it, subtract from it, divide it up. Can you use sensory input? Can somehow, can you incorporate the senses? So it seems like those would be good prompts to put into, Yes. GP, to put into AI, and ask them, okay, how would you do the opposite? How would you do this? How would you add? What can you add? How can you take one thing out of this and make that in isolation an idea? Take any process, like the process you just described, any process or template or guidance or direction can be turned into prompts that you then use one by one with AI. And so like, well, you can ask all these questions you just mentioned, and I've seen your stuff also. So like all those questions that you would normally ask yourself and think and do and so on, you can ask those questions or give those directions to AI, and it'll produce whatever you're looking for. And that's exactly how to think about it. You need to think about it as, it's like having a junior writer or a colleague that you can just endlessly ask questions to and have it repeat stuff back to you and redo and rewrite and analyze. And you can analyze text too. You can give it text and then ask it to analyze the text according to whatever framework or topic or mode that you're looking for. Like as an example, it's kind of fun to add to give it, let's say you give it text from a book and from any book, fiction book, whatever. And then you say analyze this text according to cognitive behavioral therapy framework and tell me who this patient would be. And so if you take like a fiction character from a book and you give it like, let's say a dialogue or description text from the book and you use that as a text, it'll apply cognitive behavioral therapy as a model to understand the character of the book and then it'll give you a profile of the character in the book based on CBT. And you can also do that with yourself. Like if you have text about yourself like your diary or a journal that you use, you can take your journal entries, put it into the prompt and then ask questions about the text that will tell you something about yourself. So in that sense, it's like, it can act as your junior writer, as your therapist, as your journal entry device, it can act as your creative assistant, collaborator. And I keep saying to people, and I know you get it, David, and I'm hoping other people start to get it too. What you can do is endless. The only limit to what you can do is your own imagination and what you're curious about. If generally in life, you're not curious, you don't have a lot of interest and you don't have a really any imagination, that you're not gonna get a lot out of chat GPT or GPT-3 or AI. It's your output is dependent on your input with these tools. But if you have curiosity in life and you think about whatever you're working on and you can formulate thoughts and you can put thoughts into words, then you can just use those thoughts into words to prompt AI and get stuff back. That's why I say like prompts are simultaneous, which is how you interact with, you give it prompts, like which is literally just sentences and instructions. Prompts are simultaneously worthless and valuable because I can have the same core idea in my mind, but I can express it 10, 15, 20 different ways. And so most people get stuck like, what do I say to it? How do I say it? And you only need to know a few basics to really generate your own prompts endlessly. And your creativity will be enhanced by you taking an active role in formulating your thoughts that turn into prompts that you then use in your interactions. I can give you prompts and you can Google for prompts, but they will get you only so far. What's better is for you to start thinking and acting and speaking and writing like a human, as if you're interacting with another human, even though the robot is not a human. But like, how would you talk to a junior writer if you're asking for a copy? Or how would you talk to a collaborator if you're both working on a book? Just talk to it like that and you'll get better output than if you were just copy like prompts that I give you or someone else gives you. Does that make sense? It absolutely.
